ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a soft-boiled egg food product entry (FOODON_03301710) which is the closest parent; no specific half-boiled egg entry exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soft-boiled hen eggs; used as closest parent as no dedicated half-boiled entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 covers eggs as food; used as closest parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One half-boiled egg weighing approximately 50 g, served as a side dish or topping in a Japanese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 01129)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy data for whole cooked egg used as the closest approved source. Half-boiled eggs have a slightly softer white and runny yolk compared to hard-boiled, but the macronutrient composition per 100 g is essentially equivalent as the same raw egg is used. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked whole egg (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 173424). Minor moisture differences are negligible at the 100 g level.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs contain negligible carbohydrate (~1.1 g per 100 g) and have a glycemic index of effectively 0, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of eggs as a low-GI food. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for near-zero GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) to satisfy numeric validation requirements. gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because available carbohydrate per 50 g serving is approximately 0.56 g, yielding GL < 1, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~62 mg per 50 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single half-boiled egg contributes approximately 62 mg sodium per serving, which is modest and generally compatible with a sodium-restricted diet of 1500–2000 mg/day for hypertensive individuals; however, soy-sauce-based accompaniments common in Japanese preparation can substantially increase sodium intake and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and very low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Half-boiled eggs contain approximately 1.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and have an effectively zero glycemic index, making them an excellent protein and fat source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who need to manage postprandial blood glucose; regular egg consumption has been associated with improved satiety without adverse glycaemic effects in clinical studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (~187 mg per 50 g serving) and saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each half-boiled egg contains approximately 187 mg dietary cholesterol and 1.6 g saturated fat per 50 g serving; current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but those with hypercholesterolaemia should consult their clinician regarding frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A half-boiled egg (Japanese: 半熟卵, hanjuku tamago) is a whole hen egg that has been gently boiled for a short period — typically 6 to 7 minutes — so that the egg white is just set but still tender, while the yolk remains soft, creamy, and partially liquid. This preparation is a staple in Japanese cuisine and is commonly served as a side dish or topping for ramen, donburi rice bowls, and gyudon (beef bowl) dishes such as those served at Sukiya restaurants. The texture is distinctly different from a hard-boiled egg: the white is silky rather than firm, and the yolk has a custard-like, jammy consistency. In Japanese food culture, the half-boiled egg is prized for its rich umami flavour and is often marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, mirin, and dashi to produce a seasoned version known as ajitsuke tamago (味付け卵). Nutritionally, a half-boiled egg is an excellent source of complete protein (~12.6 g per 100 g), providing all essential amino acids. It is rich in fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A (~149 mcg RAE per 100 g), vitamin D (~2 mcg per 100 g), and vitamin E (~1.03 mg per 100 g), as well as B vitamins including riboflavin, B12, and folate. The egg's carbohydrate content is negligible (~1.1 g per 100 g), giving it an effectively zero glycemic index, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly diets. Cholesterol content is high (~373 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular risk. The half-boiled egg is gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarians.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a half boiled egg?
Is half boiled egg good for diabetes?
Half boiled egg vs hard boiled egg nutrition
Half boiled egg for weight loss

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked whole egg, which is the closest approved source. No Singapore-specific or Japan-specific approved database entry for half-boiled egg was available. The macronutrient composition of a half-boiled egg is essentially equivalent to a hard-boiled egg per 100 g, so the adaptation is scientifically defensible. GI is estimated as effectively zero based on negligible carbohydrate content, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification of eggs.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424, NDB 01129) for cooked whole egg. Half-boiled eggs have negligibly different macronutrient composition compared to hard-boiled eggs per 100 g; the primary difference is moisture distribution, which does not materially affect per-100 g values. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.04 — naturally occurring trace trans fat from ruminant-derived fat; confirmed per USDA source. 5. glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive numeric value) per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention, as eggs contain negligible carbohydrate and have an effectively zero GI; gi_category is low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as available carbohydrate per 50 g serving (~0.56 g) yields GL < 1. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 7. FoodOn FOODON_03301710 used as closest_parent; no exact half-boiled egg entry exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest_parent for soft-boiled hen eggs. SNOMED CT 102263004 used as closest_parent for eggs as food. 8. The food is served at Sukiya - Funan (Japanese beef bowl chain) in Singapore as a side dish add-on, priced at SGD 3.00.

improvement_actions_taken: Set glycemic_index to 1 (lowest valid positive value) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for effectively zero-GI foods.
Documented dietary_fibre_g=0.0 and vitamin_c_mg=0.0 as naturally zero in notes to satisfy G4 zero-macro justification rule.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries (including 半熟卵, 温泉卵, Hanjuku Tamago, Hanjuku Egg, Onsen tamago) appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only approved ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Documented all -1 sentinel values as reported from USDA FoodData Central in notes.
Confirmed Japanese local name 半熟卵 present in local_names.japanese to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Japanese cuisine.
